section.title: The Man Born Blind
section.sectionRationale: The sixth sign, the healing of a man born blind, becomes an extended drama of growing sight and deepening blindness. As the once-blind man sees more and more clearly who Jesus is, from the man called Jesus, to a prophet, to one from God, to Lord, the religious leaders grow more and more blind, until they cast him out. Jesus finds the outcast, receives his worship, and pronounces the great reversal: He came so that the blind may see and those who claim to see may go blind.
section.thesis: Jesus, the light of the world, gives sight to a man born blind and, through the man's rising confession and the leaders' hardening rejection, exposes the deeper truth that spiritual sight is given to those who come to Him in faith, while those who proudly claim to see remain in their guilt.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the healing and the question of whose sin caused the blindness; the neighbors' astonishment; the Pharisees' interrogation and the parents' fear; the man's bold second testimony and his expulsion; and Jesus finding him, receiving his worship, and declaring the judgment of sight and blindness.

units.label: The sixth sign
units.note: that the works of God might be displayed

verses.text: Now as Jesus was passing by, He saw a man blind from birth,
and His disciples asked Him, “Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?”
Jesus answered, “Neither this man nor his parents sinned, but this happened so that the works of God would be displayed in him.
While it is daytime, we must do the works of Him who sent Me. Night is coming, when no one can work.
While I am in the world, I am the {{phos|light}} of the world.”
When Jesus had said this, He spit on the ground, made some mud, and applied it to the man’s eyes.
Then He told him, “Go, wash in the Pool of {{siloam|Siloam}}” (which means “Sent”). So the man went and washed, and came back seeing.

units.label: The neighbors astonished
units.note: isn't this the man who used to beg?

verses.text: At this, his neighbors and those who had formerly seen him begging began to ask, “Isn’t this the man who used to sit and beg?”
Some claimed that he was, but others said, “No, he just looks like him.” But the man kept saying, “I am the one.”
“How then were your eyes opened?” they asked.
He answered, “The man they call Jesus made some mud and anointed my eyes, and He told me to go to Siloam and wash. So I went and washed and received my sight.”
“Where is He?” they asked. “I do not know,” he answered.
They brought to the Pharisees the man who had been blind.
Now the day on which Jesus had made the mud and opened his eyes was a Sabbath.
So the Pharisees also asked him how he had received his sight. The man answered, “He put mud on my eyes, and I washed, and now I can see.”

Because of this, some of the Pharisees said, “This man is not from God, for He does not keep the Sabbath.” But others said, “How can a sinful man perform such {{semeion|signs}}?” And there was division among them.
So once again they asked the man who had been blind, “What do you say about Him, since it was your eyes He opened?” “He is a prophet,” the man replied.
The Jews still did not believe that the man had been blind and had received his sight until they summoned his parents
and asked, “Is this your son, the one you say was born blind? So how is it that he can now see?”
His parents answered, “We know he is our son, and we know he was born blind.
But how he can now see or who opened his eyes, we do not know. Ask him. He is old enough to speak for himself.”
His parents said this because they were afraid of the Jews. For the Jews had already determined that anyone who confessed Jesus as the Christ would be put out of the synagogue.
That was why his parents said, “He is old enough. Ask him.”

units.label: One thing I know: I was blind, now I see
units.note: the bold testimony and the expulsion

verses.text: So a second time they called for the man who had been blind and said, “Give glory to God! We know that this man is a sinner.”
He answered, “Whether He is a sinner I do not know. There is one thing I do know: I was blind, but now I see!”
“What did He do to you?” they asked. “How did He open your eyes?”
He replied, “I already told you, and you did not listen. Why do you want to hear it again? Do you also want to become His disciples?”
Then they heaped insults on him and said, “You are His disciple; we are disciples of Moses.
We know that God spoke to Moses, but we do not know where this man is from.”
“That is remarkable indeed!” the man said. “You do not know where He is from, and yet He opened my eyes.
We know that God does not listen to sinners, but He does listen to the one who worships Him and does His will.
Never before has anyone heard of opening the eyes of a man born blind.
If this man were not from God, He could do no such thing.”
They replied, “You were born in utter sin, and you are instructing us?” And they threw him out.

units.label: Spiritual blindness
units.note: the blind see and those who see go blind

verses.text: When Jesus heard that they had thrown him out, He found the man and said, “Do you believe in the Son of Man?”
“Who is He, Sir?” he replied. “Tell me so that I may believe in Him.”
“You have already seen Him,” Jesus answered. “He is the One speaking with you.”
“Lord, I {{pisteuo|believe}},” he said. And he worshiped Jesus.
Then Jesus declared, “For judgment I have come into this world, so that the blind may see and those who see may become blind.”
Some of the Pharisees who were with Him heard this, and they asked Him, “Are we blind too?”
“If you were blind,” Jesus replied, “you would not be guilty of sin. But since you claim you can see, your guilt remains.”

phos.gloss: Light. Jesus repeats the claim from the previous chapter, I am the light of the world, and then enacts it by giving light to eyes that had never seen. The physical healing is a parable of the spiritual sight He gives; those who come to the light see, while those who reject it are left in darkness they will not admit.

siloam.gloss: Siloam, the name of the pool, which John notes means Sent. The evangelist highlights the meaning because Jesus is the Sent One, the one sent from the Father; the man is healed by washing in the pool named Sent, a quiet sign that healing comes through the One God has sent. The pool's waters were drawn for the Tabernacles ceremony behind the earlier living water sayings.

semeion.gloss: Sign. Even some Pharisees recognize that such a sign, opening the eyes of a man born blind, could not be done by a mere sinner. The sign points beyond itself to Jesus' identity as the one from God, though the leaders finally refuse to read it rightly. The whole Gospel is built on signs meant to lead to faith.

pisteuo.gloss: To believe, trust. The man's journey is a journey into faith: from knowing only the name of Jesus to confessing Lord, I believe, and worshiping Him. Belief in John is not mere opinion but a whole-person trust that bows before Christ, and it is the fitting response to the light He gives, in contrast to the leaders' proud refusal.

basic.context: Jesus sees a man blind from birth. His disciples ask whose sin caused it; Jesus says neither, but that God's works will be displayed in him, and then, as the light of the world, He heals him with mud and a washing at the pool called Sent. What follows is a long, almost comic drama of investigation. The neighbors are baffled, the Pharisees interrogate the man twice, his frightened parents defer to him, and the man grows steadily bolder in his testimony until the leaders throw him out. Jesus finds the outcast, reveals Himself, receives his worship, and declares that He came so the blind would see and the self-assured would go blind.
basic.hermeneutics: Watch the two opposite journeys. The blind man moves from ignorance to worship, seeing more and more clearly who Jesus is. The leaders move from investigation to insult to expulsion, growing blinder as they claim to see. The key question is not the cause of suffering but the purpose God works through it, and the deeper theme is spiritual sight and blindness. Jesus turns the whole episode into a judgment: those who admit their blindness receive sight, while those who insist they see remain guilty.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on light and sight. Jesus is the phos, the light of the world, and heals through the pool of Siloam, which John notes means Sent, quietly pointing to Jesus as the Sent One. Some Pharisees admit no mere sinner could do such a semeion, a sign, yet they will not believe. The healed man, by contrast, comes to pisteuo, to believe, confessing Lord, I believe, and worshiping.
basic.typology: The washing in the pool named Sent points to cleansing and sight given through the One sent from God. The giving of sight to the blind fulfills the messianic promises of Isaiah, a sign of the age of salvation dawning in Jesus.
basic.christ: Christ is the light of the world who gives sight, the Son of Man who receives worship, and the judge whose coming divides the seeing from the blind. He seeks out the man who was cast out, showing that He gathers those the religious establishment rejects.
basic.god: God works His purposes even through lifelong suffering, so that His works may be displayed. He is not the author of the man's blindness as punishment; He is the one who turns it to glory. He listens to those who worship Him and do His will.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 35 and 42 promise that the eyes of the blind will be opened. John 8:12 declares the light of the world. 2 Corinthians 4 speaks of God shining light into blind hearts. John 3 said light divides those who come and those who hide.
basic.oneStory: The promised opening of blind eyes arrives in Jesus, the light of the world and the Sent One. As one poor beggar is given sight and led to worship while the proud are left in darkness, the story shows how the coming of the light divides humanity, gathering the humble who admit their need and hardening those who trust their own vision.

context.literary: A masterfully told narrative, structured as a series of interrogations with the once-blind man at the center, growing in boldness while the leaders harden. The healing occupies only a few verses; the bulk is dialogue, driving the theme of sight and blindness. It is framed by Jesus' declaration that He is the light of the world and closes with His verdict on spiritual blindness, forming a unit that dramatizes the earlier claim of chapter 8.
context.historical: The assumption that suffering is caused by specific sin was common, which the disciples' question reflects. The pool of Siloam, fed by Hezekiah's tunnel, has been excavated in Jerusalem. The threat of being put out of the synagogue, excommunication, was a severe social and religious penalty, and John's note about confessing Jesus as the Christ reflects the sharp conflict between the synagogue and followers of Jesus in the Gospel's setting.
context.canonical: The healing fulfills Isaiah's promise that the eyes of the blind will be opened, Isaiah 35:5 and 42:7, a sign of the messianic age. The theme of spiritual blindness connects to Isaiah 6:9-10, quoted later in John 12:40, and to 2 Corinthians 4:4-6, where the god of this age blinds minds while God shines the light of the gospel. The man's expulsion foreshadows the cost of confessing Christ, John 16:2.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present the healing as a sign that Jesus is the light of the world, to trace one man's growth from healing to worship, and to expose the spiritual blindness of those who reject the light, so that readers will admit their need and receive sight through faith in the Son of Man.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a specific healing and controversy. Its abiding teaching is that suffering can serve God's purposes, that Jesus gives spiritual sight to those who come to Him, and that proud self-sufficiency, claiming to see, leaves a person in guilt. The man's bold confession models faithful witness under pressure.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean that the man's blindness was so that the works of God would be displayed?

views.label: God's purpose, not punishment for sin
views.case: Jesus rejects the assumption that the blindness was caused by the man's or his parents' sin; instead God purposed to display His works through it, so the emphasis is on God's redemptive purpose in suffering rather than blame.

views.label: A statement about purpose, read with care about cause
views.case: The Greek can be read so that the point is not that God caused the blindness for a display, but that now, in this condition, the works of God are to be shown; either way Jesus refuses to trace it to the man's guilt.

debates.bearing: Both guard against the cruelty of blaming the sufferer and direct attention to God's power to bring glory and grace out of affliction, fulfilled as the man comes to sight and faith.

debates.question: What is the judgment Jesus speaks of in verse 39?

views.label: A present dividing by response to the light
views.case: Jesus' coming as light itself sorts people: those who admit their blindness receive sight, while those who claim to see are hardened; the judgment is the self-revealing effect of His presence now.

views.label: A sober warning of guilt that remains
views.case: The judgment falls as guilt remaining on those who proudly refuse the light; it is not that Jesus came chiefly to condemn, but that rejecting the light confirms people in their sin.

debates.bearing: Both hold together John's teaching that Jesus came to save, yet His coming inevitably judges, exposing and hardening the proud while giving sight to the humble.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter plays on light and sight. Jesus is the phos, the light of the world, and He enacts the claim by giving light to eyes that had never seen. He sends the man to wash in the pool of Siloam, and John pauses to note that the name means Sent, a quiet pointer to Jesus as the one sent from the Father, through whom cleansing and sight come. Even divided Pharisees admit that no mere sinner could work such a semeion, a sign, yet they will not believe. The healed man, meanwhile, is drawn steadily to pisteuo, to believe: from the man they call Jesus, to a prophet, to one from God, until at last he says, Lord, I believe, and worships Him.

typology.type: The opening of blind eyes promised by Isaiah
typology.fulfillment: Jesus giving sight to the man born blind, a sign of the messianic age of salvation, Isaiah 35:5 and John 9:7.
Cleansing and sight given through Jesus, the Sent One from the Father, John 9:7.
Jesus giving both physical and spiritual sight, dividing those who see from those who go blind, John 8:12 and 9:5.

study.christ: Christ is the light of the world, and this sign shows what that means: He gives sight to those who have never seen. He is the Sent One, healing through the pool named Sent, doing what only God can do. He is the Son of Man who reveals Himself to the outcast and receives his worship, something only God may rightly accept. And He is the judge whose very coming divides humanity, giving sight to those who admit their blindness and leaving in guilt those who proudly claim to see. He seeks out the man cast from the synagogue, gathering to Himself the very ones the establishment rejects.
study.god: God is shown to work His purposes even through lifelong affliction, not as punishment for the man's sin but so that His works of grace and power might be displayed. He is the God who gives sight, fulfilling the ancient promises, and who listens to those who worship Him and do His will. Yet He is also the God whose light judges: those who welcome it are saved, and those who refuse it are hardened. In Jesus, God seeks the outcast and receives the worship of the humble while the proud are left in the dark.

crossReferences.note: Then the eyes of the blind will be opened, a messianic promise fulfilled in this sign.
I am the light of the world, the claim Jesus enacts by giving sight to the blind man.
God shines light in blind hearts, while the god of this age blinds minds, paralleling the theme of sight and blindness.
They will put you out of the synagogue, the cost of confessing Christ, foreshadowed in the man's expulsion.

study.oneStory: The long-promised opening of blind eyes arrives in Jesus, the light of the world and the Sent One from the Father. As a poor beggar is given sight and led step by step to worship, while the learned and powerful grow blinder in their pride, the episode dramatizes the whole story of the gospel: the coming of the light divides humanity. It gathers the humble who admit their need and receive sight, and it hardens those who trust their own vision and will not come. The one cast out by the synagogue is found and welcomed by the Son of Man, a sign of the new community He is gathering to Himself.

quiz.q: How does Jesus answer the question of who sinned to cause the man's blindness?

options.text: Neither the man nor his parents; it happened so that the works of God would be displayed in him.
options.note: Verse 3 rejects the blame and points to God's purpose.

options.text: The man himself sinned before birth.
options.note: Jesus explicitly says neither the man nor his parents sinned to cause it.

options.text: His parents' sin was the cause.
options.note: This was a common assumption of the day, but Jesus rejects it; the blindness was not punishment for their sin.

options.text: No one's sin caused it, and God had a purpose in it.
options.note: True in substance, but it omits the specific goal, that the works of God would be displayed in him.

quiz.q: How does the healed man's understanding of Jesus develop through the chapter?

options.text: From the man called Jesus, to a prophet, to one from God, to Lord whom he worships.
options.note: His confession grows through the interrogations until he believes and worships.

options.text: He believes fully and completely from the very first moment.
options.note: His faith grows step by step; he does not begin with full understanding.

options.text: He never really decides who Jesus is.
options.note: He ends by confessing Lord, I believe, and worshiping Jesus.

options.text: He comes to call Jesus a prophet and one from God.
options.note: True of the middle of his journey, but it stops short of his final confession and worship.

quiz.q: What was the man's simple, unshakable testimony to the Pharisees?

options.text: One thing I know: I was blind, but now I see.
options.note: Verse 25 records his plain, powerful witness.

options.text: He mainly retold how Jesus had put mud on his eyes and healed him.
options.note: He did recount what happened, but his boldest and simplest testimony was the plain fact, I was blind, now I see.

options.text: He admitted he was not sure anything had changed.
options.note: He was certain: I was blind, but now I see.

options.text: He insisted that Jesus must be from God to do such a thing.
options.note: He does argue this later, but his simplest testimony is the personal fact, I was blind, now I see.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by coming for judgment so the blind see and the seeing go blind?

options.text: Those who admit their blindness receive sight, while those who proudly claim to see remain guilty.
options.note: Verses 39 to 41 explain the reversal in terms of humility and pride.

options.text: Jesus came mainly to condemn the world.
options.note: His coming does bring judgment, but John 3:17 says He came to save; condemnation is not His chief purpose.

options.text: Physical blindness is a sign of God's rejection.
options.note: The chapter teaches the opposite; the physically blind man is the one who truly sees.

options.text: His coming divides people by how they respond to the light.
options.note: True, but it omits the sharp point that the proud who claim to see are left in guilt.

quiz.q: Why were the man's parents afraid to speak about the healing?

options.text: Because anyone who confessed Jesus as the Christ would be put out of the synagogue.
options.note: Verse 22 gives the reason, fear of excommunication.

options.text: Because they preferred that their grown son speak for himself.
options.note: They did say he is old enough, ask him, but John tells us the real reason was fear of being put out of the synagogue.

options.text: Because they doubted he had been healed.
options.note: They confirmed the facts; it was fear of the leaders that made them defer to him.

options.text: Because they feared the religious authorities.
options.note: True, but it does not name the specific threat, being put out of the synagogue for confessing Christ.

questions: The disciples wanted to know whose fault the blindness was; Jesus pointed instead to what God would do through it. How might God be at work in a hardship you are tempted only to blame?
The healed man simply said, I was blind, but now I see. What is the plain, personal testimony of what Jesus has done in your life?
Under pressure the man grew bolder while his parents grew silent. Where is fear of others' disapproval keeping you from speaking honestly about Jesus?
Jesus sought out the man after he was thrown out. How does it comfort you that Christ draws near to those rejected by others?
The proud who claimed to see stayed blind. Where might a confident sense that you already see be keeping you from seeing Jesus more truly?

translationNotes.note: Siloam means Sent, as John notes; the detail points to Jesus as the Sent One from the Father, through whom sight and cleansing come.
Do you believe in the Son of Man; the BSB footnote records a variant, the Son of God, in some manuscripts. Either title calls the man to faith in Jesus.
Being put out of the synagogue was a severe penalty of exclusion; John's note reflects the sharp conflict between the synagogue and confessors of Christ.
The BSB footnote gives the literal wording and notes that some manuscripts do not include the man's words Lord, I believe, and his worship in verses 38 and 39a; the sense of the passage is unchanged.

Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
